apercu: The kingdom of Urartu (Biainili in Urartian, the biblical "kingdom of Ararat") united the highlands around Lake Van under Sarduri I around 860 BC. Assyria's great rival, it excelled in hydraulics — the 72-km Menua canal still irrigates the Van region —, in bronze metallurgy and in fortress architecture. King Argishti I founded the fortress of Erebuni there in 782 BC — the birth of Yerevan, 29 years older than Rome — under the protection of the national god Haldi.
Weakened by Cimmerian and Scythian raids, the kingdom collapsed around 590 BC before the Medes. It is on this substrate that the Armenian people emerges into the sources.
contexte: Permanent rivalry with Assyria: campaigns of Shalmaneser III, sack of the holy city of Musasir by Sargon II in 714 BC.

capitales.info: Capital founded by Sarduri I on a rocky spur above Lake Van. The citadel preserves the kings' cuneiform annals and the royal rock tombs.
Fortress founded in 782 BC by Argishti I on Arin Berd hill. Yerevan, its descendant, is thus 29 years older than Rome.
Royal residence of Rusa II (7th c. BC) near Tushpa, the kingdom's last great foundation.

pouvoir: Median then Achaemenid empire · Orontid dynasty
apercu: After Urartu's fall the plateau passed to the Medes, then to Achaemenid Persia. The trilingual Behistun inscription (520 BC) mentions the name "Armina" for the first time.

The local Orontid (Yervanduni) dynasty, firmly attested from the late 5th century BC (Xenophon met satrap Orontas there in 401 BC), governed the satrapy, which paid 400 talents of silver and supplied 20,000 colts a year for the feasts of Mithra, according to Herodotus (Histories, III, 93). Xenophon and the Ten Thousand crossed its snowy villages in 401 BC.
contexte: First imperial occupation: Armenia is a province (satrapy) integrated into the Persian system of royal roads and tribute, but administered by an Armenian dynasty.

capitales.info: The old Urartian fortress became the satrapal seat: Achaemenid silver rhytons and a columned apadana hall were found there.
Former Urartian Argishtikhinili, residence of the Orontids in the Araxes plain — future first capital of the independent kingdom.

apercu: After Gaugamela (331 BC), satrap Orontes (Yervand) became independent in fact as Alexander's empire broke apart: Armenia was never really conquered by the Macedonians.

The Orontids reigned as kings between the Seleucids to the south and Pontus to the north, in measured Hellenisation — the rocks of Armavir bear Greek inscriptions quoting Euripides and Hesiod. Around 200 BC Antiochos III deposed the last Orontid and entrusted the country to his generals Artaxias and Zariadres — who would soon betray him.
contexte: An imperial interlude: real independence under theoretical Seleucid suzerainty, acknowledged by intermittent tribute.

capitales.info: First royal capital; Greek inscriptions carved on its rocks quote Euripides and Hesiod.
Founded by Yervand IV at the confluence of the Araxes and Akhurian, "on steep rocks", last Orontid capital.

pouvoir: Artaxiad dynasty · apogee under Tigranes II the Great
apercu: In 189 BC, after Antiochos III's defeat by Rome, Artaxias I proclaimed himself king and, according to Strabo, "gathered the lands where one and the same language was spoken, Armenian".

Apogee under Tigranes II the Great (95–55 BC): allied to Mithridates of Pontus, he conquered Syria, Cilicia and Atropatene and proclaimed himself "king of kings" and founded around 83 BC his imperial capital, Tigranakert, peopled with deported Greeks. His empire stretched from the Caspian to the Mediterranean — before Lucullus (69) and Pompey (66 BC) reduced it to Greater Armenia.

The dynasty ended with the deposition of Queen Erato around 12 AD. Rome and Parthia then contested the throne through proxy kings for half a century (12–66 AD), until Tiridates I's founding compromise inaugurated the Arsacid dynasty.
contexte: Armenia becomes the buffer state disputed between Rome and Parthia — a role it would keep for seven centuries.

capitales.info: Founded c. 176 BC on the Araxes — by tradition on the advice of Hannibal, then a refugee at court. The Romans nicknamed it "the Armenian Carthage".
Imperial capital founded c. 83 BC by Tigranes, peopled with Greeks deported from Cappadocia. Taken and sacked by Lucullus in 69 BC.

pouvoir: Arsacid dynasty, between Rome and Persia
apercu: In AD 66 Tiridates I, brother of the Parthian king, received the crown of Armenia from Nero in Rome: a founding compromise — Parthian dynasty, Roman investiture.

In 114, Emperor Trajan invaded Armenia, deposed King Parthamasiris and annexed it as a Roman province — the only time in its history — just as the Roman Empire reached its greatest territorial extent (116). His successor Hadrian abandoned the province by 117-118 and restored a client kingdom.

In 224, the Sasanian Ardashir I overthrew the Parthian Arsacids: Arsacid Armenia, the dynasty's sole surviving branch, became a prime target of Sasanian hostility. Around 252, King Khosrov I was assassinated on the orders of the Sasanian Shapur I, who then imposed decades of Persian domination — an episode that, according to Agathangelos, founded the family vendetta that would later drive his son Tiridates III to reclaim the throne. In 301, this same Tiridates III, converted by St Gregory the Illuminator, made Christianity the state religion: a world first. In 405 the monk Mesrop Mashtots created the Armenian alphabet, still in use. In 387, Rome and Sasanian Persia partitioned the kingdom — about a fifth in the west for Rome, the rest under Persian sway. The monarchy was abolished by Persia in 428, at the nakharars' own request.
contexte: Partition of 387: Rome and Sasanian Persia divided the kingdom — about one fifth in the west for Rome, the rest under Persian influence.

capitales.info: Capital in the 3rd century and spiritual seat: Etchmiadzin cathedral, founded 301–303, is among the oldest in the Christian world.
Former Artaxiad capital, still a great merchant city; a Roman-Persian treaty regulated the silk trade there.
Founded c. 335 by Khosrov III; the late political capital, later seat of the catholicoi and of Persian and Arab governors.

apercu: In the east, Sasanian Persia governed Persarmenia through a marzpan (viceroy) seated at Dvin; in the west, Byzantium administered its Armenian provinces reorganised by Justinian (Armenia I–IV).

In 451, the Persian attempt to impose Zoroastrianism provoked the battle of Avarayr: a military defeat for Vardan Mamikonian but a moral victory — the treaty of Nvarsak (484) guaranteed the Armenians' religious freedom. In 591 the frontier moved east in Byzantium's favour.
contexte: A kingless age: real power lay with the great nakharar families and the Church, which broke with Constantinople at the council of Dvin (554).

capitales.info: Seat of the marzpan and, from 485, of the catholicosate. A great caravan city, repeatedly destroyed by earthquakes and sieges.
Byzantine stronghold on the invasion road, founded in the 5th century — today's Erzurum.

apercu: The first Arab raids reached Dvin as early as 640. Around 700 the caliphate organised the province of Arminiya — Armenia, Georgian Iberia and Caucasian Albania — governed by an ostikan from Dvin, then Bardaa. In 705, to break the nobility, the Arabs burned hundreds of nakharars alive in the churches of Nakhichevan and Khram.

The nakharar revolts (703, 748, 774–775 — crushed at Bagrevand) bled the old Mamikonian and Kamsarakan houses… to the profit of the more prudent Bagratids, who accumulated titles, lands and the office of "prince of Armenia" until they regained the crown.
contexte: Occupation by integration: tribute and Arab garrisons, but the Christian princes' autonomy was recognised by treaty (Muawiya's pact, 652).

capitales.info: Seat of the ostikan, with mosque, customs and mints striking "Arminiya" dirhams. The city remained the catholicos' seat too.

apercu: In 884 Ashot I Bagratuni was crowned "king of the Armenians", recognised by both caliph and Byzantine emperor. Ani became the capital in 961: the "city of 1001 churches", a Silk Road stage of perhaps 100,000 inhabitants.

The kingdom split into sister realms — Vaspurakan (908), Kars (963), Lori (978), Syunik (987) — easy prey: in 1021, King Senekerim Artsruni of Vaspurakan, threatened by the first Turkish raids, ceded his kingdom to Byzantine emperor Basil II in exchange for lands in Cappadocia — a negotiated cession, not a conquest, occurring just as Basil II campaigned in the Caucasus against Georgia (1021-1022). Byzantium then annexed Ani (1045, under Constantine IX, ceded by the last king Gagik II); the Seljuks sacked Ani in 1064 and crushed Byzantium at Manzikert (1071), opening Anatolia to the Turks.
contexte: A golden age of architecture (Ani, Aghtamar, Sanahin, Haghpat) against a background of fatal political fragmentation.

capitales.info: Capital from 961, between ravines and double walls. Its cathedral (1001) is the work of Trdat — the architect called to repair the dome of Hagia Sophia.
Island palace of king Gagik Artsruni: the church of the Holy Cross (915–921) and its biblical reliefs are masterpieces of medieval art.
Bagratid capital from 928 to 961, then seat of the kingdom of Vanand until its cession to Byzantium.

pouvoir: Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia · Zakarid principality · Mongols
apercu: Fleeing the Seljuk invasions, Armenian princes settled in Cilicia on the Mediterranean: the Rubenid principality (1080) became a kingdom when Leo I the Magnificent was crowned in 1198. Allied to the Crusaders and then to the Mongols, the state prospered through its ports — Ayas, described by Marco Polo.

On the highlands, the Zakarian brothers, generals of Queen Tamar of Georgia, liberated the north (1199–1236) before the Mongol wave. The decline of the Mongol Ilkhanate after 1335 left Cilicia without its protector: the strategic port of Ayas was lost as early as 1347, and Sis, the shrunken capital, fell to the Mamluks in 1375 after a month-long siege. Captured, the last king Leo V of Lusignan was imprisoned in Cairo, then freed in 1382 through Castilian-Aragonese mediation; he wandered Europe before dying in exile in Paris in 1393 — his tomb is at Saint-Denis. With the fall of Sis the last independent Armenian state died out: none would rise again until 1918.
contexte: Armenia now existed in two places: a maritime kingdom in Cilicia, and a highland passed from the Seljuks to the Mongols (1236) and then to Turkmen hordes.

capitales.info: Capital of the kingdom of Cilicia and seat of a catholicosate that remained until 1930. Taken by the Mamluks in 1375 after a long siege.
A merchant renaissance under the Zakarians, then slow decline under the Mongols; an earthquake (1319) and shifting trade routes finished the city.

pouvoir: Ottoman Empire (west) · Safavid then Qajar Persia (east)
apercu: After the battle of Chaldiran (1514, under Selim I) Ottomans and Safavids fought over the plateau. Suleiman the Magnificent and Safavid shah Tahmasp I sealed the peace of Amasya (1555), which drew a first lasting border — Erzurum and the west to the Ottomans, Erivan and the east to the Persians — which the peace of Zuhab (1639) would only confirm and stabilise 84 years later, after another round of wars. In 1604 Shah Abbas deported hundreds of thousands of Armenians to Isfahan — New Julfa, which would dominate the silk trade as far as India.

In 1828 the treaty of Turkmenchay ceded the khanates of Erivan and Nakhichevan to Russia: the territorial matrix of modern Armenia. From it would come the First Republic (1918) and independent Armenia (1991).
contexte: Four centuries without a state: the Church (Etchmiadzin, restored 1441) and the merchant diasporas became the nation's backbone.

capitales.info: Seat of the Persian khanate, a city of fortresses, mosques and gardens on the Hrazdan. Its capture in 1827 earned Paskevich the title "Count of Erivan".
The Holy See, reinstalled in 1441, the only pan-Armenian institution: its encyclicals and collections linked Constantinople, Isfahan, Venice and Madras.
